topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow, sharp and sudden. I found the laptop open on the kitchen counter, the screen glowing with half-finished prose. My breath hitched. It wasn't just a journal; it was a novel. And the protagonist? Every curve of my face, every stupid habit I possessed, meticulously documented in prose. A slow burn started in my chest, a tight, hot knot of refusal. I walked over, my movements stiff, and picked up the nearest page. The words swam before my eyes, intimately familiar yet utterly alien. "Chapter Three: The Unyielding Shadow." I slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing in the sudden silence. I didn't look at him. I just turned and walked toward the living room, my jaw set hard enough to crack stone.

[story 2]

I watched him laugh that evening, the sound warm and easy, and the realization settled in my gut like cold lead. The manuscript, tucked beneath a stack of old poetry books, was undeniable proof. He hadn't just been daydreaming; he had been crafting a narrative centered entirely on my existence. A strange, brittle energy rose up inside me. I leaned against the doorframe, crossing my arms so tightly my knuckles turned white. When he finally looked up, his expression shifted to confusion, and I met it with a steady, unreadable gaze. "That's… a lot of work," I managed, my voice flat, deliberately devoid of any softness. I didn't move to confront him; instead, I simply began tidying the bookshelf with aggressive, precise movements, refusing to acknowledge the tremor running through my hands.

[story 3]

The evidence lay spread across his desk: annotated drafts, character sketches, and pages filled with my own mannerisms described with startling accuracy. A strange, almost giddy sense of power bubbled up, quickly hardening into something cold and immovable. I felt a distinct tightening around my throat, a need to push back against the very air in the room. I didn't speak immediately. I simply walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, feeling the tension coil deep in my shoulders. When I finally turned back, my eyes held a challenging glint. "You know," I said, my tone dangerously level, "I think you've reached the end of your story." I didn't wait for a reply, just turned my back on the evidence and walked out of the room, leaving him alone with his narrative.
